segments.s771-g193: تو (you) is the 2nd-person singular addressee. این را places 'this' as the definite object (را marks it). چنین modifies خردکاری and here means 'such/so' (i.e. 'such cunning/cleverness'). خردکاری is a noun meaning an artifice/cunning or prudence. مدان is an archaic imperative/prohibitive form meaning 'do not consider/suppose' or 'do not call (it)', so the whole segment is a negative instruction: 'do not consider this thus cleverness.'
segments.s771-g194: چو here means 'when' (a temporal subordinating particle). چیره شدی is a two-word verbal idea: چیره (dominant/overmastering) + شدی (you became) — a temporal perfect/past form used to mean 'when you become/master.' کام مردان is a nominal phrase 'the desires/wishes of the men (heroes)', and بران (= بر + آن contracted) is an adverbial/locative 'for/on that' or 'toward that.' The clause has an implied copula/predication (i.e. 'will be'), so the sense is 'when you become dominant, the men's wishes will be for that.'
segments.s771-g195: گر is the conditional particle 'if.' از دامن او is a prepositional phrase 'from his garment/hem.' درفشی کنند is a verbal phrase: درفشی here denotes 'a banner/standar(d)-thing' (i.e. 'make into/raise as a banner') and کنند is the 3rd-person plural verb 'they do/make.' Together: 'if they make a banner from his robe.'
segments.s771-g196: تو را marks 'you' as the definite direct object. با سپاه is an instrumental/accompanying phrase 'with the army.' از introduces the source 'from' and is followed by بنه (here part of a locative/idiomatic phrase indicating place/seat/foundation — in practice 'from your post/position'). برکنند is the 3rd-person plural verb 'they remove/dismiss/uproot.' The whole means 'they will, with the army, remove you from (your position).' Note that را is the object marker tying تو to برکنند.
segments.s771-g1: چو is a temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing a clause. ماهوی is an epithet describing the person (here 'moon‑like' / 'moon‑faced') and functions as the subject; دل را is the definite object with the Persian object marker را. برآورد is a perfect/past verbal form ('caused/turned/made'), and گرد is an archaic/poetic verbal particle here that completes the verb phrase (sense of 'turned' / 'thereupon became'). Together the verb phrase means 'turned/moved (his) heart.'
segments.s771-g2: بدانست is a past tense verb 'he knew/realized.' The following clause is a content clause introduced elliptically by کو (here functioning like an archaic/subordinating particle 'that/there' with an existential sense). نیست negates existence ('is not / there is none'), جز is the preposition 'except,' and یزدگرد is the proper name (the only one except whom exists). The whole subordinate clause reports what he realized.
segments.s771-g3: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him' (he said to him). گفت here is the verb 'said' (classical past) introducing reported speech. بشتاب is an imperative 'hasten!' or 'make haste!' and زین انجمن = ز + این انجمن (poetic contraction) means 'from this assembly' or 'out of this meeting.'
segments.s771-g4: هم‌اکنون is an adverb 'right now / immediately.' جدا کن is an imperative verb phrase 'separate / cut off' (جدا = apart, کن = do! imperative). سرش را is the definite object 'his head' (سر + possessive -ش, plus object marker را). ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' (ز = from, تن = body).
segments.s771-g5: وگرنه is a conditional adverb 'otherwise / if not.' هم‌اکنون again means 'at once / immediately.' ببرم is a 1st‑person verb form (subjunctive/present with modal force) 'I will take / I will cut off' and is followed by the object سرت 'your head' (سر + second‑person possessive -ت). The clause is a threat introduced by the conditional.
segments.s771-g6: نمانم (here functionally equivalent to classical نماند) expresses non‑existence or lack of survivors 'will not remain / there will not remain.' کسی is the indefinite 'anyone / anyone at all.' زنده is the predicate/adjective 'alive.' از marks source or provenance 'from/of,' and گوهرت is 'your jewel' (گوهرت = گوهری + -ت). The clause means 'no one alive will remain from/connected to your jewel.'
segments.s771-g7: شنیدند is past plural 'they heard.' ازو is the prepositional phrase 'from him.' این سخن is the direct object 'this speech / these words.' مهتران is a plural noun 'the chiefs/elders' (an epithet for leading men) and here is one of the groups who performed the verb; word order is somewhat inverted (verb before subject) which is common in poetry.
segments.s771-g8: بزرگان is a plural noun 'great ones / elders' (appositive to مهتران). بیدار functions as an adjective or nominal 'the awake/alert' and is coordinated with کنداوران 'the investigators / excavators' by و 'and.' The phrase lists the groups who heard the speech; adjectives/nouns here stand in apposition.
segments.s771-g57: 'ز خون کیان' is a prepositional phrase ('ز' = poetic 'از', 'from/of') modifying 'شرم دارد'. 'شرم دارد' is a present-tense predicate 'has shame / is ashamed'. In this poetic word order the noun 'نهنگ' (the subject) is placed after the verb phrase for emphasis: literally 'from the Kians' blood shame has the whale.'
segments.s771-g58: This is a conditional clause + main clause. 'اگر کشته بیند' = 'if (he) sees a slain (one)' where 'کشته' is a past participle used as 'the slain' (object) and 'بیند' is the classical present/subjunctive form 'he sees'. The main clause 'ندرد پلنگ' = 'the leopard does not tear' with the negated present verb 'ندرد' and the subject 'پلنگ' placed after it; the object ('him/it') is understood from 'کشته'.
segments.s771-g59: 'ایا' marks a rhetorical question. 'بتر از دد' = 'worse than an enemy' ('بتر' comparative 'worse', 'از' = 'than/of'). 'به مهر و به خوی' is a pair of prepositional phrases ('به' = 'with/in', 'مهر' = 'affection/kindness', 'خوی' = 'disposition/temper') describing the respects in which something is worse; the repeated 'به' is a poetic parallelism.
segments.s771-g60: 'همی گاه' is a time adverbial 'at times / sometimes' (poetic inversion). 'شاه' is the subject 'the king'. 'آیدت' is a verb with the enclitic object 'ت' ('comes/befalls you'), and 'آرزوی' is the noun 'wish/desire' functioning as the thing that 'comes to you' (object). Poetic word order places the verb + clitic before the noun object.
segments.s771-g61: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'بر دست ضحاک' is an agent phrase 'by Zahhak's hand' (the preposition 'بر' + possessor), 'جم' is the patient/name 'Jam', and 'کشته شد' is a passive past 'was killed' (compound of participle + 'شد'). Word order here places the agent phrase before the patient, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s771-g62: 'چه مایه' is an exclamatory phrase 'what an amount/what a cause' intensifying the following noun. 'سپهر' is the subject 'the sky/heavens'. 'از برش' here means 'on account of him / because of him' (poetic fused preposition + enclitic) and 'گشته شد' is a compound past meaning 'was turned / became / underwent' — together expressing an exclamatory result the heavens experienced because of that event.
segments.s771-g63: 'چو' again marks a temporal 'when'. 'ضحاک' is the subject, 'بگرفت' is the past verb 'seized/took hold of' (prefixed past tense 'بـ+گرفت'), and 'روی زمین' is a locative phrase 'on the face of the earth / upon the land' (روی = 'surface/face' + زمین = 'earth'). The verb precedes the locative complement in this poetic line.
segments.s771-g64: 'پدید آمد' is a compound verb meaning 'appeared/ came into being' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'). 'اندر جهان' is a locative phrase 'in the world' (poetic 'اندر' = 'in'), and 'آبتین' is the proper name of the person who 'appeared'. Word order places the verb before the locative and the name after it for metrical and emphatic reasons.
segments.s771-g33: به + proper name + گفت is a prepositional phrase 'to Mahuy' (به ماهوی) followed by the past-tense verb گفت ('he said'). ای introduces a vocative 'O' addressing the addressee; ستمگاره مرد is a doubled vocative/epithet ('oppressor man') with the noun مرد reinforcing the address. Word order is typical older Persian: indirect object (به ماهوی) + verb + vocative.
segments.s771-g34: چنین 'thus' modifies the imperative that follows. از ره 'from/off the way' (راه = ره) marks the path referenced; پاک‌یزدان is an epithet for the deity and here names the moral/religious path. مگرد is an archaic/poetic negative imperative ('do not turn/withdraw') — the phrase as a whole commands 'do not thus turn away from the godly path.'
segments.s771-g35: همی is the habitual/progressive particle placing the action in an ongoing sense ('I keep/always see'). تیره is an adjective 'dark/ominous' modifying دل و هوش 'heart and awareness'; بینم is 1st person present (classical form) 'I see.' The coordinated direct objects دل و هوش follow the verb, typical of Persian poetic order.
segments.s771-g36: Again همی + بینم gives 'I still see.' خار 'thorn' (literal or figurative) is the direct object; در آغوش تو is a prepositional phrase 'in your embrace' placed after the verb-object, specifying location/possession. Word order: particle + object + verb + locative phrase is idiomatic in older Persian poetry.
segments.s771-g37: A string of coordinated adjectival phrases describing the (implied) person: تنومند 'stout/robust' and بی‌مغز 'brainless' with جان نزار 'emaciated soul/life.' Note the negative prefix بی- in بی‌مغز which forms an adjective 'without brain.' The sequence uses و to link qualities.
segments.s771-g38: همی again marks ongoing action. دود ز آتش کنی is a two-part predicate: دود 'smoke' with ز آتش 'from the fire' and کنی 'you make/produce' (2nd-person present in classical Persian) — literally 'you make smoke from the fire.' خواستار follows as a predicate adjective/noun 'desirous' describing the same subject. The verb کنی agrees with the person being addressed.
segments.s771-g39: تو را uses را as the definite/object marker placing 'you' as the affected party. زین جهان is a fused poetic locative ('from this world' / 'of this world') and serves to locate سرزنش 'reproach' which appears as the object seen by بینم 'I see.' آز ('greed') is appended as a separate noun indicating cause or accompaniment; the clause is elliptical, common in poetry, so nouns can stand without an explicit verb linking them.
segments.s771-g40: ببرگشتنت is a nominalized verb phrase 'your return' used adverbially/nominally (the initial ب- here is the prefixed particle used in classical usage to form temporal/adverbial sense: 'at/when your return' or simply 'your return'). گرم و رنج گداز are two coordinated adjectives/compounds: گرم 'warm/fervent' and رنج گداز 'pain-consuming / burning-sorrow' (گداز as an adjectival noun 'melting/burning' used figuratively). Together they describe the state connected to your return.
segments.s771-g113: کزین is a frozen prepositional compound meaning "from this/therefore"; بدنشان is a noun + enclitic pronoun (بدن + شان) meaning "their body/bodies" with را omitted because this is a poetic phrase; دو گیتی is a compound meaning "the two worlds/both worlds" (دو modifies گیتی); شوی is the present/subjunctive/optative form of شدن (here "become") used poetically, so the whole clause reads literally "from this their bodies become (the) two worlds."
segments.s771-g114: چو is the conditional/temporal particle here meaning "if/when"; گفتار is the noun "speech/words"; دانندگان is the plural agentive "those who know/experts/wise men"; نشنوی is the present subjunctive/negative second-person form of شنیدن (ن + شنوی) meaning "you do not hear/heed." Together this clause gives the protasis: "if you do not heed the words of the wise."
segments.s771-g115: چو here is the conditional/temporal "if/when"; کاری is the noun "a task/thing to be done" (the provided lexical senses did not list this nominal sense, so sense selection is marked accordingly); که introduces a relative clause "that" qualifying کاری; امروز is the adverb/noun "today/nowadays"; بایدت is باید + encliticِ ت ("it is necessary for you / you must"); کرد is the past/infinitive form of کردن used here to mean "do/make" within the relative clause — the clause reads "a task that you must do today."
segments.s771-g116: به فردا رسد is a verbal phrase meaning "is/gets deferred to tomorrow" (به + فردا + رسد — رسد is 3rd-person singular of رسیدن "to reach/arrive"); ز (poetic form of از) here means "from/because of;" و functions as the connective "and/then/so"; برآرند is a 3rd-person plural present form (برآوردن/برآرندن variant) meaning "they bring/collect/raise"; گرد is a postpositive adverb/noun meaning "around/about" ("gather about/around it"). The clause therefore says roughly, "if it reaches tomorrow, then because of that they will gather about it."
segments.s771-g117: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle often glossed "indeed/just/ever" here giving emphasis; یزدگرد is a proper name (Yezdgerd); شهنشاه is an apposition/title "king of kings/sovereign"; را is the definite-object/ topical marker attaching to the whole NP. Grammatically this chunk presents the object/topic that will be contrasted in the following phrase.
segments.s771-g118: بتر here functions in an older/poetic comparative sense (compare به‌تر) and combines with خواهی (second-person singular of خواستن, "you will/would want/choose") to yield a comparative idea "you will prefer/choose X rather than Y"; از marks the comparison "from/of/than"; ترک is "Turk"; بدخواه is a descriptor "ill-wisher/hostile"; and the final را again marks the contrasted NP. Syntactically the phrase means "you will (prefer) X to the hostile Turk."
segments.s771-g119: که is the relative pronoun "who/which/that" introducing a descriptive clause; در جنگ is the prepositional phrase "in battle"; شیرست is an adjectival/poetic compound (شیر + -ست) meaning "lion-like/brave" (the copula is omitted in poetry); بر گاه شاه is a locative phrase "in the presence/at the court of the king" (بر + گاه = "before/at the place of" + شاه). Together it describes the person as "who in battle is lion-like before the king."
segments.s771-g120: درخشان is an adjective "shining/brilliant"; به here functions as a prepositional/comparative marker "in/with/as" linking درخشان to the following nominal phrase; کردار is a noun "deed/conduct" which is being qualified; تابنده is a participial adjective "radiant/shining"; ماه is a simile head "the moon." Grammatically the phrase means "shining in conduct like the radiant moon," completing the relative description.
segments.s771-g17: ازین دو (از + این) = “of/from these (two)”; یکی را is marked by the object marker را, so the whole phrase means “one of these two” as the direct object. همی‌بشکنی is a 2nd-person singular present/future-like form with the archaic/prosodic prefix همی- (habitual/ongoing or emphatic future); the verb appears after its object (typical Persian V-final order in poetry).
segments.s771-g18: روان و خرد را is a coordinated noun phrase (‘soul and wisdom’) with the object-marker را applying to the whole phrase. به پا افگنی is a directional/resultative phrase: به پا = “to/at the foot/ground,” and افگنی is the 2nd-person singular present form of an older verb (cf. افکندن → افکنی) meaning “you cast/throw/bring down”; the verb again follows its object phrase, producing the sense “you cast … down.”
segments.s771-g19: نگر is an imperative meaning “look/consider.” تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose or scope here: تا چه گویی = “to see/so that (one knows) what you will say” (چه = “what”). گویی in the manuscript list is anomalous (lemma given as گفت‌وگو etc.), but here functions as a verb form ‘you say/will say’; the clause precedes the main imperative بپرهیز. ازین = ‘from this/these’ (از + این).
segments.s771-g20: مشو بدگمان is a negative imperative: مشو (don’t become) + بدگمان (suspicious/doubtful) = “do not be suspicious.” با introduces the target/agent of the injunction: با جهان‌آفرین = “with the World‑Creator” i.e. do not be suspicious of the Creator. Note the negative imperative مشو formed from شدن.
segments.s771-g21: نخستین is an adverbial/introductory word meaning “first.” ازو = از + او (“from him/it”). بر تو آید گزند is the verbal clause: آید (comes/happens) with گزند (harm) as its noun; Persian poetry often places the verb with its noun complement at the end, so the whole means “first, harm will come upon you from him.”
segments.s771-g22: به introduces an indirect/directional relation here (often rendered “to/against” or “upon”); فرزند مانی = “Mani’s child” (possession: noun + name). یکی کشتمند = “a certain killer/murderer” (یکی = ‘one/a certain’ modifying the substantive کشتمند, an older form meaning ‘murderer’). The phrase reads as an appositive/result: “a murderer will (be) to Mani’s child,” i.e. “a murderer will strike Mani’s child.”
segments.s771-g23: که is the subordinating conjunction “that.” بارش = بار + ش (“its burden/load”) — the possessive clitic ش attaches to بار. کبست is an archaic/poetic noun here (textual/lexical uncertainty; likely a calamity/ill omen). آید is the verb “will come/happen.” و links برگ خون (“a leaf/page of blood”) as a parallel/result phrase; the clause therefore says that a burden (of kabast/ruin) will come and a ‘page of blood’ (poetic image) will appear.
segments.s771-g24: به زودی is an adverbial phrase “soon.” سر خویش is “his/her/one’s own head” where خویش is formed from خوی + ش (the reflexive/possessive element ش); بینی here is 2nd-person singular present/future form of ‘دیدن’ (you will see/you see) — note that بینی can also mean “nose,” but context and verb form show it is verbal here. نگون is a predicative adjective “overthrown/downcast,” so the clause means “soon you will see your own head overthrown.”
segments.s771-g185: وگر introduces a conditional ('if'); بریزی is the 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive/optative used in a protasis ('if you should spill'), so the clause means 'if you spill'. خون او را is the definite object (خون = 'blood', او = 'his') marked by را. بدست is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'by (your) hand' (the possessive pronoun is implied), indicating the agent/instrument of the action.
segments.s771-g186: که here gives the reason/explanation ('for/because'); کین‌خواه او در جهان is a nominal phrase 'his avenger in the world' (کین‌خواه = 'avenger', او = 'his', در جهان = 'in the world'), and ایزدست is the predicate 'is God' — ایزد + ست (archaic/poetic contraction of است) yields 'is God'. The whole clause states the causal warning: 'for his avenger in the world is God.'
segments.s771-g187: چپ و راست is an idiomatic adverbial phrase 'left and right' = 'on every side' or 'everywhere'. رنجست is a predicative nominal with the archaic/political copula -ست (رنج + ست = 'there is suffering'), and the following nouns و اندوه و درد are coordinated additions to the predicate ('and sorrow and pain'), listing the afflictions present.
segments.s771-g188: نگه کن کنون is an imperative: نگه (a verbal noun 'look/watch') + the imperative کن 'do' yields 'look now' (کنون = 'now'). تا introduces a result/indirect-question sense here ('so that / in order to see'), and چه بایدت کرد is an older word order for an indirect question: چه = 'what'; بایدت = باید + enclitic -ت 'to you' ('what must/ought to be done to you'); کرد is the verbal element 'do' (past stem used here to form the periphrastic construction meaning 'will be done' / 'shall be done').
segments.s771-g189: پسر گفت is the reporting clause 'the son said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly; باب here functions as a proper name or title addressed by the son (in the text it is used vocatively), followed by the epithet فرخنده‌رای ('auspicious/fortunate-minded') modifying that name. In context باب is best read as a vocative name/title despite the dictionary senses given.
segments.s771-g190: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('when' or 'if'); دشمن کنی is 'you make (someone) an enemy' with the object omitted (understood from context). ز + و here is the poetic contraction meaning 'from him' (ز = 'from', و = an enclitic pronoun 'him/it'); بپرداز is an imperative/hortative form (from پرداختن) meaning 'drive away / remove / deal with', and جای is the noun 'place' — together: 'if you make him an enemy, drive him away from that place.'
segments.s771-g191: سپاه آید is a simple verb phrase 'troops/army will come' (آید = present/future 'come'). او را uses را to mark the indirect object 'to him' (i.e., 'come to him'). ز ما indicates origin 'from us'. چین و چین is a poetic reduplication meaning 'fold upon fold' or 'in successive ranks' (adverbial intensifier: repeatedly / in layers). The order is typical poetic Persian (Subject + Verb + indirect object).
segments.s771-g192: به ما is the dative 'for/to us'; بر شود is a periphrastic passive/impersonal formation (بر + شود) meaning 'becomes upon / befalls' or simply 'becomes' with an adversative nuance; تنگ روی زمین is the predicate 'the face/surface of the earth becomes narrow' — an idiom meaning 'the earth becomes cramped for us' (i.e., there will be no room, we will be pressed). The clause thus says the consequence: the earth will become narrow for us.
segments.s771-g129: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The clause reads literally 'when you were chief in the country': تو (you) is the pronoun, بود is the past copula 'was', مهتر is the predicate noun 'chief', and به کشور is a prepositional phrase 'in the country' (به used here as 'in'). بسی is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'much/very' or 'indeed' attached to the clause.
segments.s771-g130: نکرد is the past negative 'did not do' (3rd person plural implied); it governs the following object. اینچنین qualifies رای as 'such' (i.e. 'such a counsel/decision'), رای is the noun 'opinion/counsel', and the sentence-final combination هرگز کسی ('never anyone') is the negative quantifier: with the negative verb, هرگز + کسی yields 'no one ever'.
segments.s771-g131: چو again marks 'when'. بهرام is a proper name (Bahram). چوبین here is the epithet 'Chubīn' attached to the name (etymologically 'club-like/with a club'), and که begins a relative clause ('who / who had …') linking to the following numeric phrase; the numeric words that follow are outside the provided word-list but belong to that relative clause.
segments.s771-g132: عناندار is an adjective 'stubborn' describing the following noun. و is the coordinating 'and'. برگستوانور is an opaque/compound descriptive term (here transmitted as a single lexeme in the list) attached to سوار; سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman'. Together the phrase characterises the army attached to Bahram as 'stubborn and [compound-term] horsemen.'
segments.s771-g133: به here functions instrumentally 'by/with' (i.e. 'with one arrow'). یک تیر is the instrumental phrase 'one arrow'. او is the third-person pronoun 'him/he' (object or reference), پشت is the noun 'back', and برگاشتند is a past plural verb meaning 'turned/returned' (here used to indicate turning of backs or retreating). The verb is plural so the subject is plural (they).
segments.s771-g134: بدو is the dative 'to him/against him'. دشت پیکار is a compound meaning 'the plain of battle' (dشت = plain, پیکار = battle), and بگذاشتند is a past plural verb 'they placed/they set' — here 'they set (the) battlefield' or 'they set about battle' directed toward the referent noted by بدو.
segments.s771-g135: چو 'when' introduces another temporal clause. از رای شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the counsels of the kings' (از marking source or cause). سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive suffix ش), سیر گشت is the verbal phrase 'became sated/was satisfied' (سیر = satiety, گشت = became), i.e. 'he grew weary/sated.'
segments.s771-g136: سر دولت is a genitival/construct phrase 'the head/top of (his) state/fortune' (سر = head/top, دولت = sovereignty/fortune). روشنش is an adjective 'bright/illustrious' with the possessive suffix 'his' attached, and زیر گشت is a compound verb meaning 'became under (i.e. became subject/subdued)'; together the phrase says that his bright rule/head was brought low or made subject.
segments.s771-g153: شبان‌زاده را دل پر از تخت بود = literal Persian order: topic marked by را comes before the predicate. 'شبان‌زاده را' puts the shepherd‑born as the person concerned; دل 'heart' here means desire/longing, modified by the adjectival phrase 'پر از تخت' ('full of the throne'). بود is the past copula 'was'. The whole clause means 'for the shepherd‑born the heart was filled with thoughts/desire of the throne.'
segments.s771-g154: ورا پند آن موبدان سخت بود = ورا is an old/poetic dative 'to him'; پند 'admonition/advice' is the subject of the predicate سخت بود 'was harsh/strict'. آن موبدان 'those mobeds' is a genitive/attributive phrase identifying whose advice it was. The clause literally reads 'To him the advice of those mobeds was severe.'
segments.s771-g155: چنین بود تا بود = a taut, proverbial construction: 'it was thus as long as it was' where تا functions as 'as long as'/'until' linking two instances of بود ('was'). و این تازه نیست then adds a present comment: 'and this (situation) is not new.' Note the switch from past narrative (بود) to general present (نیست) for the aphoristic comment.
segments.s771-g156: که کار زمانه بر اندازه نیست = که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/because/that'): 'the business/ways of time' (کار زمانه) is the subject, and بر اندازه نیست is a copular negation meaning 'is not according to measure' i.e. 'is not regular/just.' The preposition بر + اندازه is idiomatic: 'in accordance with measure.'
segments.s771-g157: یکی را برآرد به چرخ بلند = 'یکی را' (one person, marked by را) is the object; برآرد is an older 3rd‑person singular (present/habitual or gnomic present) of برآوردن meaning 'raises/puts up'; به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to the high wheel/heaven' (چرخ standing for the celestial wheel or fate). The subject (the wheel/fate) is implied rather than stated.
segments.s771-g158: یکی را کند خوار و زار و نژند = parallel to the previous clause: یکی را (another person) is the object; کند is the present/habitual 3rd‑person singular of کردن 'makes'; three adjectives joined by و describe the result: خوار 'despised', زار 'wretched', نژند 'base/lowly'. Together: 'it makes one despised, wretched and base.'
segments.s771-g159: نه پیوند با آن نه با اینش کین = correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. The first coordinated noun phrase is 'پیوند با آن' (a tie/connection with that), the second is 'با اینش کین' (hatred toward this); note اینش is an older/clitic form meaning 'this (of him/it)'. There is no finite verb in this short clause—the copula 'was/had' is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s771-g160: که دانست راز جهان‌آفرین = که here introduces the reason/explanatory clause 'for/because'; دانست is past tense 'he knew' and takes راز 'secret' as its object; جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' (the Creator). So: 'for he knew the secret of the Creator of the world.'
segments.s771-g65: بزاد is an archaic past verb placed before its subject (verb-first order common in epic Persian) meaning 'was born'. آفریدون is the proper name (the subject). فرخ نژاد is a compound epithet (adjective + noun) 'fortunate-born / of happy lineage' modifying the name.
segments.s771-g66: جهان را is the definite object marked with را ('the world'). یکی دیگر is the subject phrase 'one other / another'. آمد and نهاد are two past verbs in sequence (poetic coordination): آمد 'came' + نهاد (past of نهادن) 'placed/established', so 'another came and set/established the world.'
segments.s771-g67: شنیدی is 2nd-person past/perfect used rhetorically as 'have you heard'. که introduces the content clause ('that / who'). ضحاک بیدادگر is the noun plus epithet: the proper name Zahhak followed by بیدادگر 'the oppressor/tyrant'.
segments.s771-g68: چه is the interrogative 'what'. آورد is past 'brought/caused' (here 'brought about' or 'caused to happen'). از آن خویشتن را is a complicating object phrase: از 'from/of', آن 'that', خویشتن 'self' with را as object marker, and به سر is a locative idiom 'upon the head' meaning 'upon himself'. Together: 'what did he bring upon his own head.'
segments.s771-g69: برو here is a formulaic transitional particle ('thus' / 'so') used in poetry rather than a literal imperative. سال بگذشت is 'years passed' (بگذشت is past of گذشتن 'to pass' applied to time). مانا qualifies 'هزار' (inverted order) so the sense is 'a thousand lasting/enduring years.'
segments.s771-g70: به فرجام کار is a prepositional/time phrase 'to the outcome/end of the affair'. آمدش is 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic -ش = 'to him'). خواستار is 'a claimant / petitioner' (here the noun/agent): the word order in poetry places the verb before its subject, so 'a claimant came to him.'
segments.s771-g71: و simply connects this line to the previous. دیگر 'the other' identifies a second figure; که introduces the following appositive/relative clause. تور is the proper name Tūr, and آن سرافراز مرد is an emphatic apposition 'that lofty/honored man' modifying Tur (demonstrative آن + adjective سرافراز + noun مرد).
segments.s771-g72: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. آز ایران is a genitival phrase 'greed for Iran' (آز = greed, governed by ایران). ورا is the pronominal clitic 'to him/against him' and رنجه کرد is the compound 'caused distress' (رنجه 'suffering / distress' + کرد 'did/caused'). The clause asks where, out of greed for Iran, he caused suffering.
segments.s771-g177: برهنه شد is a passive/resultative construction: برهنه (exposed) + شد (became/was), so ‘was exposed/laid bare’. این راز من (‘this secret of mine’) is the patient/topic placed after the verb for poetic word order. در جهان is a locative phrase (‘in/to the world’) modifying where the secret was exposed.
segments.s771-g178: شنیدند is a past-tense plural verb ‘they heard’. یکسر here is an adverb (‘at once/all together’). کهان و مهان are coordinated nouns after the verb and indicate classes of people (literally ‘… and …’); together the phrase means that everyone (high and low/various people) heard it.
segments.s771-g179: بیاید here has an optative/irrealis force ‘may it be that…/would that…’. مرا (clitic split in the word list) is the accusative ‘me’ (object). از بدش indicates cause/agent ‘because of his evil’ (بد + enclitic ش = ‘his evil’). جان به سر is an idiomatic juxtaposition where جان (life) is linked to به سر (lit. ‘to the head’) to mean loss/end of life or being ruined — the whole phrase expresses that the speaker’s life be taken/ruined by that evil.
segments.s771-g180: نه … نه … is the standard ‘neither … nor …’ negation. تن ماند (‘body remained’) has ماند (remain) as the verb; ایدر is the locative ‘here’. بوم و بر are the two coordinated nouns (‘dwelling/homeland’ and another term for place/land), so the clause states that neither body nor home/place will remain here.
segments.s771-g181: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb ‘thus/so’. داد is the past-tense verb ‘gave’ and takes پاسخ (‘answer’) as its object; خردمند مرد (‘the wise man’) is the agent (adjective + noun). Word order is poetic but straightforward: adverb – verb – object – agent.
segments.s771-g182: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause ‘that…’. این خود is emphatic (‘this very thing’). نخستین is an adjective/noun ‘first’, and نبایست is the negative of بایستن (‘ought/should’), so نبایست کرد expresses that it ought not to be done; کرد is the lexical verb ‘do’ placed at the end as the clause’s main verb.
segments.s771-g183: اگر introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). شاه ایران شود uses شود (subjunctive/present-future ‘becomes’) after the noun phrase ‘king of Iran’. دشمنت (‘your enemy’) is the subject/topic that follows the protasis for emphasis; the clause sets the condition under which the consequence follows.
segments.s771-g184: ازو (‘from him/of him’) marks the source/agent of the evil. بد رسد uses رسد in the sense ‘befall/happen to’ (here ‘evil will befall’), with بی‌گمان (‘undoubtedly’) as an adverb of certainty. بر تنت is a prepositional phrase ‘upon your body’ (بر + تنت) marking the target of the misfortune.
segments.s771-g81: جهاندار کیخسرو is a title + name in apposition: 'the world-ruler Ki Khosrow.' از پشت اوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from behind him' where از is the preposition, پشت is the location 'behind/back', and اوی is an archaic/genitive pronoun form 'his/him'. The whole segment supplies the subject and its originating place.
segments.s771-g82: بیامد is a past verb 'came.' جهان کرد is a second verbal phrase where کرد (causative/do) takes جهان ('the world') as its object, and پر گفت‌و‌گوی is a predicate/complement meaning 'full of conversation' (پر = 'full (of)'). Poetic word order places جهان before کرد; the two verbs (came, made) are sequential actions of the same subject.
segments.s771-g83: نیا را is the definite direct object (را marks the object). به خنجر uses به instrumentally ('with a dagger' — poetic use of به for means), and به دو نیم کرد is a causative construction: کرد 'made/did' with the result phrase 'into two halves' (به دو نیم). Overall: 'he made Nia into two halves with a dagger.'
segments.s771-g84: سر کینه‌جویان is a genitive phrase 'the heads of the kinekjooyan (revenge-seekers)' where کینه‌جویان is a plural agent-noun. پر از بیم کرد is a causative/resultative phrase: پر از بیم 'full of fear' and کرد 'made' — 'caused the heads to be full of fear.' The verb کرد governs the resulting state.
segments.s771-g85: چهارم is the ordinal 'fourth' modifying سخن کین ('speech/word of vengeance' or, idiomatically, 'one of the vengeful speakers/words'). ارجاسپ بود is the nominal predicate 'was Arjasp' with بود as the past copula. Poetic word order places the descriptive noun phrase before the copula.
segments.s771-g86: که introduces a relative clause 'who...' here. ریزنده is an agentive participial noun 'the one who sheds/causes to flow,' خون is the object 'blood' (with لهراسپ as its possessor: 'Lohrasp's blood'), and بود is the copula 'was.' The clause literally says 'who was the blood‑shedder of Lohrasp.'
segments.s771-g87: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' اسفندیار is the subject, اندر آمد is a compound meaning 'entered/arrived within,' and به جنگ indicates the destination 'into/onto battle.' The whole segment means 'when Esfandiyar came into battle.'
segments.s771-g88: ز کینه is an ablative/instrumental 'out of/for hatred.' ندادش is the past negative verb 'did not give' with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'him' as its object. زمانی درنگ is a noun phrase 'a time (of) delay' ('a moment's pause'); placed after the verb as its object. Together: 'out of hatred he did not give him even a moment's delay.'
segments.s771-g73: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic determiner here meaning 'that/the same' pointing back to a previously mentioned person. ایرج پاک‌دین را is a definite object phrase: پاک‌دین is an epithet 'pure-in-faith/devout', and را is the Persian definite-object marker marking Iraj as the patient. بکشت is an archaic past third-person transitive ('he/they killed'); the b- prefix is old narrative morphology but the function is simply past tense 'killed'.
segments.s771-g74: برو here functions in a narrative sense 'thereupon/then' (formally an imperative-root form used as a connective in poetry). گردش آسمان is a reversed genitive: گردش 'turning/rotation' with آسمان 'heaven/sky' as its domain. شد is the past copula/verb 'became/was', and درشت is a predicate adjective 'harsh/rough/violent'; together they mean 'the heavenly rotation became violent.'
segments.s771-g75: منوچهر is the proper name (subject). زان تخمه is a prepositional-phrase 'from that seed/stock' (زان = 'from that', تخمه = 'seed/offspring'). آمد پدید is a two-word predicate: آمد 'came' (simple past) and پدید 'forth/appeared' — a poetic compound meaning 'came into being/appeared'.
segments.s771-g76: شد is the past/copulative 'became/it happened'. آن بند بد را is a definite object phrase ('that bad/evil bond' marked by را). سراسر is an adverb/quantifier 'entirely/throughout' that scopes over the following noun. کلید here is used poetically in a light verbal/nominal sense 'to be unlocked/loosed' (i.e. the bond is undone). Together the clause states that the evil bond was wholly released.
segments.s771-g77: دیگر here functions as a list/contrastive marker 'another/next' (part of an enumerative sequence: 'another was ...'). سیاوش is the personal name (subject). ز تخم کیان is a genitive phrase 'from the seed/ofspring of the Kians' (ز = 'from', تخم = 'seed/origin', کیان = the Kayanian royal house).
segments.s771-g78: کمر بست is a light-verb idiom: کمر 'waist/loin/girdle' plus بست (past of بستن) 'bound' meaning 'girded his loins / prepared himself'. بی‌آرزو is an adjectival phrase 'without hope/hopeless' modifying the action, and در میان is a locative phrase 'in the midst/in the middle'. Word order is typical Persian: verb-final clause with preposed locatives/adverbs.
segments.s771-g79: به گفتار is an instrumental/circumstantial phrase 'by/according to the words/counsel of...'; به here marks the source or instrument. گرسیوز افراسیاب names the adviser and the actor (Garsivaz's words, Afrasiab is the doer); the phrase as a whole indicates that what follows is done on Garsivaz's counsel.
segments.s771-g80: ببرد is a past-tense transitive verb 'he took away/took off' (برد with the narrative prefix b-). از introduces the things removed 'from (him)': روان و خرد 'soul/spirit and wisdom/mind' as coordinated nouns, followed by شرم و آب 'shame and water' (آب used figuratively for life/purity). The structure lists the items taken away by the subject.
segments.s771-g41: کنون is a temporal adverb placed at the front meaning “now.” زندگانیت is the possessive form (زندگان + -یت + enclitic -ت) meaning “your life/being”; Persian older/poetic morphology attaches the possessor after the noun. ناخوش is a predicative adjective “unwell/unhappy,” and بود is the past copula (‘was’), so the clause literally reads “Now your life was unhappy.”
segments.s771-g42: چو رفتی is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = “when” and رفتی is the past second/third-person form ‘you left/when you went.’ نشستت is the noun نشست with the possessive enclitic -ت (“your seat/your place/sitting”); در + آتش is a locative phrase “in fire/in flames,” and بود is again the past copula (‘was’). The clause order is subordinate (چو رفتی) followed by the main clause (نشستت در آتش بود).
segments.s771-g43: نشست here is a past action verb “sat (down)” (classical/poetic past form), followed by the pronoun او for emphasis. The conjunction و links a second clause: شهروی (a proper name) بر پای خاست is an idiom meaning “rose to his feet” or “stood up.” Note the verb خاست is an archaic past for “arose/stood.”
segments.s771-g44: به ماهوی گفت places the speech-act verb before the quoted words: به + name = “to Māhūy” (he addressed Māhūy) and گفت is the past ‘said’ (here used to introduce direct speech). این دلیری چراست is the quoted question: این = “this,” دلیری = “daring/valour/rashness,” and چراست is an interrogative formed from چرا + است (“why is it?”). The order shows reported speech introduced after گفت.
segments.s771-g45: شهنشاه را uses را to mark the person affected (not a direct object in modern terms) — literally “upon the shah.” کارزار آمدی uses آمد (to come/happen) in the past so the phrase means “a battle came upon/befell the shah.” The verb is in a classical past form and the phrasing is idiomatic: an event ‘comes’ upon someone.
segments.s771-g46: ز is the poetic form of از meaning “from.” ز خان و ز فغفور specifies the sources: “from the Khan and from the Faghfur (the title for the Byzantine emperor).” یار آمدی means “allies/companions came.” The clause lists where the allies came from; word order places the source phrases before the verb (common in epic diction).
segments.s771-g47: ازین تخمه = ‘from this stock/lineage’ (ازین is the poetic spelling of از این). بی‌کس is a postpositive descriptor meaning “without kin/alone/destitute.” بسی یافتند is a past-tense clause “they found many” (بسی = ‘many/much’ in classical usage, یافتند = ‘they found’). The whole segment means “from this lineage they found many who were without kin.”
segments.s771-g48: که introduces a relative clause modifying بسی (“many”) — “whom/that.” هرگز is the adverb “never.” بکشتنش is the verbal substantive بکشتن with the enclitic -ش (“to kill him/them”), and نشتافتند is the negative past of شتافتن (here ‘they did not hasten’/‘they did not rush’). Together the clause literally says “whom they never hurried to kill,” i.e., they never once rushed/attempted to kill them.
segments.s771-g137: فرآیین is a noun meaning 'custom/usage' and begins a subordinate clause introduced by که (a relative/temporal conjunction here, 'when/that'). تخت بزرگی is a noun phrase 'the great throne' (تخت 'throne' + بزرگی 'greatness/grandeur'), and بجست is a past tense (classical/poetic) verb meaning 'sought' (the subject is omitted and supplied from context). The whole segment = 'as was the custom when he sought the great throne.'
segments.s771-g138: نبودش is a fused negative copular idea 'it was not' (نبود + -ش as an enclitic pronoun/placeholder); سزا here carries the sense of 'fitness/desert' ('it was not fitting/appropriate'). دست بد را is a marked object phrase ('the bad/evil hand' with را as the definite object marker), and بشست is a past-tense verb 'washed.' Together the clause expresses 'it was not fitting that an evil hand should wash (it).' Note the subject of بشست is omitted (impersonal or contextually supplied).
segments.s771-g139: بران گونه is an adverbial phrase 'for that reason / in that manner' (بران = 'on/for that', گونه = 'manner/way'). بر + کشته شد expresses a passive: کشتـه شد is 'was killed' with شد functioning as the auxiliary/passive marker. زار و خوار are predicative adjectives 'wretched and despised.' The segment = 'for that reason he was slain, wretched and despised.'
segments.s771-g140: گزافه is an adverb/adjective meaning 'exaggerated/vainglorious/empty' and modifies the imperative بپرداز ('attend to / give / pay' — here an exhortation). زین روزگار is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'this world/this age' (زین = 'this' in classical usage + روزگار 'time/age/world'). The phrase functions as an admonition: 'do not (implicitly: be taken by / rely on) the empty show of this world' — the poetical force is admonitory about worldly vanities.
segments.s771-g141: بترس is an imperative singular 'fear / be afraid' addressed to the addressee. از is the preposition 'of/from' introducing the object of fear, and خدای جهان‌آفرین is a nominal phrase 'God, the world-creator' (جهان‌آفرین is a compound meaning 'creator of the world'). The segment = 'Fear God, the Creator of the world.'
segments.s771-g142: که is the relative/conjunctive 'who/that' introducing a clause describing God. تخت آفریدست uses the classical perfect/passive-form آفریدست 'was created/has created' (here active sense: 'has created the throne'), and the coordination و تاج و نگین lists additional created items ('and the crown and the gem'). Together: 'who has created the throne and the crown and the gem.'
segments.s771-g143: تن خویش is 'your body/yourself' composed of تن 'body' plus خوی + ش (the element خوی + the possessive enclitic ش = 'your own'); بر خیره is a locative/phrase 'before the staring/onlooking ones' (بر = 'before/on', خیره = 'those who stare' or 'the astonished onlookers'). رسوا مکن is a negative imperative 'do not make (yourself) disgraced' (مکن = 'do not'). The whole = 'Do not disgrace yourself before the onlookers.'
segments.s771-g144: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/that.' بر تو is 'upon you' (preposition + pronoun), سر آرند is a verbal phrase where سر 'head' is used idiomatically and آرند is 3rd-person plural present 'they bring/raise' (i.e. 'it will be brought/raised'); زود is the adverb 'soon,' and این سخن 'this speech/this talk' is the object. Together: 'for this speech will soon be raised/used against you.'
segments.s771-g97: Topic + vocative + object phrase: تو (you/thou) is the topical/vocative subject ('son' is addressed next). زین here is an archaic prepositional/constr. form (originally زِ این = 'from/of this') introducing the following noun phrase هرچ کاری ('every/whatever undertaking'); پسر is a vocative ('son') placed after the noun phrase for address; بدرود is an interjection/imperative-like formula 'farewell' used elliptically as the verb/action directed at the addressee. Word order is emphatic: topic (تو) — prepositional marker (زین) + object — vocative — farewell formula.
segments.s771-g98: Predicate clause about Time: زمانه is the subject 'the age / time'; زمانی is an adverbial 'for a while' / 'at times'; همی‌نغنود is a present/imperfective-form verb with the older prefix همی (continuous/emphatic) + root نغنود meaning 'sleeps' or 'lies idle'. The clause states a habitual/continued state of Time.
segments.s771-g99: Imperative clause: بپرهیز is the classical imperative/present-stem verb with prefix ب- meaning 'refrain/beware'; زین again is the archaic marker 'from/this' (zِ-in) introducing the object; گنج آراسته is a noun + adjective construction where آراسته ('adorned') follows the noun (normal Persian adjective order). The whole segment is an instruction to avoid the named thing.
segments.s771-g100: Coordinated noun phrase: وزین here functions adjectivally ('dignified, grave') modifying مردری ('manliness, manly bearing'); تاج ('crown') and این خواسته ('this desire') are conjoined by و ('and'). The initial و‑ in وزین is not the simple conjunction alone but part of a descriptive sequence: 'and this dignified manliness, the crown, and this desire' — items to be shunned. Adjectives regularly follow the nouns they modify in this register.
segments.s771-g101: Habitual action clause: همی is the frequentative/emphatic prefix/adverb 'continually/still'; سر (head) is the direct object placed before the verb for emphasis; بپیچی is the present/imperfective 2nd-person form (prefix ب- + verb پیچیدن 'to turn') meaning 'you turn' or 'you bow/submit' here; به فرمان دیو is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the command of the demon' (فرمان is governed by به + genitive-like دیو). Together the clause says 'you continually turn your head at the demon's command.'
segments.s771-g102: Second coordinated clause: ببری is the present 2nd-person form of بریدن ('you cut' / 'you sever'); همی again marks continuous/habitual aspect; راه گیهان is a noun phrase 'the way/path of the world' with گیهان ('world') in apposition; خدیو at the end is a vocative/address ('O Khedive' / 'O ruler'). Syntax pairs two present habitual actions: submitting to the demon and cutting the world's path, addressed to the ruler.
segments.s771-g103: Prepositional + relative clause: به چیزی ('about/regarding something') opens the phrase; که introduces a relative clause; بر تو نزیبد is verb phrase with preposition بر ('on/upon') + pronoun تو and نزیبد (classical form n- + زیبیدن/زیبیدن) meaning 'suits/befits' (here negative: 'does not suit you'); همی at the end signals continuation/emphasis linking to the following clause. Word order places the relative clause after its antecedent as usual.
segments.s771-g104: Main negative-knowledge clause: ندانی is the 2nd-person present negative form 'you do not know' (classical present with negative prefix n-); که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; دیوت (demon) is the subject of the following verb; فریبد is a present tense verb 'deceives' (the older / poetic present form, with همی sometimes added after to mark continuous action). The sentence says you are unaware that the demon is deceiving you.
segments.s771-g89: به + ordinal introduces a temporal point: 'به پنجم' = 'on the fifth (time/utterance)'. 'سخن کین' is a genitive phrase 'word/utterance of vengeance' (سخن = speech/utterance; کین = vengeance). 'هرمزد شاه' follows as a proper name with title (apposition: 'Hormozd, the king' or the king named Hormozd).
segments.s771-g90: چو = 'when'. The sequence 'پرویز را گشن شد دستگاه' is inverted: 'پرویز را دستگاه' = 'the court/establishment of Parviz' (را marks the topical/possessive relation), 'گشن' = 'hungry' used predicatively, and 'شد' is the past of شدن 'became'. So: 'when Parviz's court became hungry.'
segments.s771-g91: به introduces indirect objects/recipients: 'به بندوی و گستهم' = 'to Banduy and Gostaham'. The verb کرد appears twice: the first کرد is the main past tense 'he did', and the second combines with آنچ ('that which / whatever') to mean 'he did whatever (he did)' — a repetitive/ emphatic structure common in epic diction.
segments.s771-g92: نیاساید is a negative present/optative form meaning 'does not / may not rest.' 'این چرخ گردان' is a noun phrase 'this revolving wheel' (چرخ often = wheel of fate). ز + گرد = 'from/by (its) turning' or 'from the round'; together: 'this revolving wheel does not rest from turning.'
segments.s771-g93: چو = 'when'. 'دستش شد' literally 'his hand became' = idiomatically 'when he got them in his hand / when he had them under his power'. The clause 'او جان ایشان ببرد' = 'he (او) took (ببرد) their lives (جان ایشان)' — ببرد here expresses the action 'took' in the narrative past sense.
segments.s771-g94: در کینه = 'in hatred' or 'by reason of hatred'. را is the object/topic marker attached to the following noun phrase; خوار used as a substantive 'the humiliated / the despised'. نتوان (negative past/capability) + شمرد ('to count') = 'cannot be counted.' So roughly: 'the humiliated in hatred cannot be numbered.'
segments.s771-g95: تو را is the experiencer 'to you' (تو + را topical/object marker). زود 'soon'. یاد آید = 'comes to mind / you remember' (یاد = remembrance, آید = comes). این روزگار is the subject 'this time/age'. Word order is topical and typical of classical Persian: 'To you soon this time will come to mind.'
segments.s771-g96: بپیچی is a 2nd-person verb form meaning 'you turn' / 'turn away' (imperative/subjunctive nuance). ز + اندیشه = 'from thought', and نابکار qualifies اندیشه: 'wicked thought.' So the clause advises 'turn away from wicked thought.'
segments.s771-g169: ز لشگر بخوانیم داننده بیست is a compact clause: ز لشگر (ز + noun) means 'from the army'; بخوانیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/epic-form of خواندن used here as 'we summoned/called' (poetic usage of بخواندن = to call out); داننده بیست is a postposed numeral phrase 'twenty knowers/experts' where the adjective/noun follows the verb phrase.
segments.s771-g170: This clause begins with بد+دان (بدان) functioning as an imperative 'know (this)'; تا introduces the content clause 'that...'; بدین بر is a short adverbial/relational phrase ('for/over this' — بدین = 'to/for this' in classical usage, followed by the preposition بر), and نباید گریست is an impersonal negative modal 'one must/ought not to weep' (نباید + infinitive/masdar-form). Word order is imperative → complementizer → circumstantial phrase → impersonal modal.
segments.s771-g171: برفتند دانندگان از برش is a past action with verb برفتند 'they departed/went away' followed by the agent/subject دانندگان 'the knowers'. از marks origin 'from', and برش is an old/poetic compound meaning 'his presence' or 'his court' (here 'from his presence/court'), so the whole phrase means 'the knowers departed from his court/side.'
segments.s771-g172: بیامد یکی موبد از لشکرش is a simple past narrative: بیامد 'came' (3rd-singular past), followed by یکی موبد 'a certain mobed' (one/more vaguely 'a certain priest'), and از لشکرش 'out of his host/army' marking origin. The verb precedes the subject for narrative compactness (common in epic diction).
segments.s771-g173: چو بنشست introduces a temporal clause ('when he/they sat down'); چو here = 'when'. ماهوی با راستان puts the two participants together: ماهوی (an honorific/collective adjective for the assembly/leaders in poetic diction) + با 'with' + راستان 'the righteous' (plural noun). Word order: temporal conjunction → verb → noun phrase + with‑phrase.
segments.s771-g174: چه بینید گفت اندرین داستان is a compressed reporting clause: چه بینید 'what (he) had seen' (چه introducing the object, بینید as a past/perfective notion 'saw'), گفت 'he said/told' (narrative past), and اندرین داستان 'in this tale' (an adverbial locating the report). The verb گفت takes the preceding clause as its direct object: 'he told what he had seen.'
segments.s771-g175: اگر زنده ماند تن یزدگرد is a conditional protasis: اگر 'if' + adjective زنده 'alive' + past verb ماند 'remained' describing the state of تن یزدگرد 'the body/person of Yezdegerd'. The order is conditional marker → predicate → subject (tense is past narrative used in condition).
segments.s771-g176: ز هر سو برو لشکر آیند گرد gives the apodosis: ز هر سو 'from every side' (prepositional phrase), برو here is an archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly 'toward' or part of the unit 'برو لشکر' (i.e. 'armies that approach'), آیند 'come' (present/plural or narrative sense 'would come/arrive'), and گرد 'around/about' giving a circumstantial sense. Together: 'armies would come from every side.'
segments.s771-g9: همه انجمن گشت ازو پر ز خشم is structured as Subject — Verb — prepositional phrase: همه انجمن گشت (the whole assembly became). گشت is the past of شدن ('became'). ازو is the poetic contraction of از او ('because of / toward him'). پر ز خشم is an adjectival phrase 'full of anger' built from پر + ز + noun (ز = 'of'), placed after the verb phrase.
segments.s771-g10: زبان پر ز گفتار و پرآب چشم strings two descriptive noun phrases. زبان (here as 'tongue' in the sense of speech) is modified by پر ز گفتار ('full of speech/words' — same construction as above). و links this to پرآب چشم, a compound adjective (پرآب = 'full of water' / 'teary') modifying چشم. Word order places the noun before its following adjectival phrase.
segments.s771-g11: یکی موبدی بود رادوی نام is a simple past existential clause: یکی ('a certain') + موبدی ('a mobad/priest') + بود (past 'was'), followed by the name رادوی with نام ('named Raduye'). The order is standard: indefinite/qualifier + noun + copula + name phrase.
segments.s771-g12: به جان و خرد برنهادی لگام places the object لگام ('bridle, restraint') with the verb برنهادی (past 'he placed upon'). The phrase به جان و خرد marks the targets ('upon/over his life and his wisdom'); note the preposition به before the two coordinated nouns. The verb is transitive past, so the whole means 'he set/put a bridle on his soul and reason' (i.e., restrained them).
segments.s771-g13: به ماهوی گفت is the indirect-object construction 'to X he said' (به + noun). گفت is the past tense 'he said'. ای بداندیش مرد is a vocative: ای introduces direct address, بداندیش is an adjective 'ill‑minded/malicious' and مرد is the noun 'man' (vocative phrase 'O ill‑minded man'). Word order places the indirect object clause before the quoted/vocative address.
segments.s771-g14: چرا دیو چشم تو را تیره کرد is a direct question in past tense. چرا = 'why'. دیو is the agent/subject ('a demon'). چشم تو را is the definite object ('your eye') with را marking it; the possessive pronoun تو follows چشم. تیره کرد is the compound verbal phrase 'made dark' (تیره = 'dark' as predicate + کرد 'did/made'). The verb comes at the end as typical in Persian.
segments.s771-g15: چنان دان که is an imperative/hortative: چنان 'so/thus' + دان (imperative or imperative-like 'know/consider'), introducing a content clause with که ('that'). شاهی و پیغمبری are two abstract nouns joined by و: 'kingship and prophethood'. The clause invites the listener to take the following statement as true.
segments.s771-g16: دو گوهر بود در یک انگشتری is a nominal predication in the past tense: دو (the numeral 'two') modifies گوهر ('gems/jewels'), بود is the past copula 'was/were', and در یک انگشتری is the locative prepositional phrase 'in one ring'. The image compares two qualities to two gems set in a single ring.
segments.s771-g25: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle (here roughly 'thus' or 'indeed'); دین یزدان is a genitival phrase 'the faith/religion of God'; شود is the present/future-like passive/auxiliary 'become / will be'; ز + و together mark source/reason 'because of him' (poetic contraction z‑u); تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/corrupt'. Word order is Subject (دین یزدان) + verb (شود) + cause (زو) + predicate (تباه).
segments.s771-g26: همان 'that same' points back to the agent just mentioned; برتو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'upon you/against you'; نفرین is the noun 'curse'; کند is the simple verb 'does/makes' (here future/habitual sense); تاج و گاه is a coordinated object 'crown and (period/of) rule' — تاج = crown, گاه = 'time/occasion' (here meaning rank/authority). The verb نفرین کند takes برتو as its indirect object.
segments.s771-g27: برهنه شود is a passive/resultative construction 'become exposed/naked'; در جهان is the locative 'in the world'; زشت تو places the predicate adjective 'shameful/indecent' with the addressed pronoun تو ('you') — literally 'you become exposed in the world shamefully.' The verb شود governs the resultant adjective برهنه.
segments.s771-g28: This clause names an agent and the act: پسر is 'son'; بدرود here functions as a proper name (not the lexical sense 'farewell'); بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'; کشت is the perfect/past sense 'killed' (noun sense 'cultivation' is not used); تو is the object 'you'. Word order is Agent (پسر بدرود) + adverb (بی‌گمان) + verb (کشت) + object (تو).
segments.s771-g29: یکی introduces an indefinite 'a certain / someone'; دین‌وری here reads as a nisba/place‑name 'of Dinavar' (not the abstract 'piety'); بود is the past tense 'was'; یزدان‌پرست is a compound noun 'God‑worshipper / pious person'. The sequence is subject phrase (یکی دین‌وری) + copula (بود) + apposition (یزدان‌پرست).
segments.s771-g30: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'; نبردی is the verb بردن in negative/historical form here meaning 'put / set / take' (i.e. 'took up, engaged in'); به بد کار دست is an idiomatic phrase 'hand to evil deed' = 'engage in wicked acts' (به = to, بد = bad, کار = deed, دست = hand/act). Together: 'who never set his hand to bad deeds.'
segments.s771-g31: که again introduces a relative/explanatory clause; هرمزد here functions as a personal name element (not the deity sense); خراد and بد are name-elements forming the compound name (خراد = 'wise', بد is the second element); نام او literally 'his name'. The phrase orders name‑elements before 'نام او' = 'his name was ...'.
segments.s771-g32: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'in this' (ب = to/into + دین = religion/faith), اندرون 'within/inside' marks the inner state, بود is the past copula 'was', آرام is the predicate adjective 'calm/at peace', and او is the pronoun 'he'. The clause means 'within this faith he was at peace.'
segments.s771-g121: یکی here functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain' (not the numeral 'one'); یادگاری is a concrete noun 'memento/inscription' describing the object; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; ساسانیان is the plural proper noun 'the Sasanians' (source of the relic). Together they form 'a memento from the Sasanians.'
segments.s771-g122: که is a relative pronoun 'which/that/who' linking this clause to 'یادگاری'; چون is the comparative 'like/as'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/him'; نبندد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بستن ('to bind/gird') used generically ('does not / would not bind' — a common epic generalization); کمر here refers to the girdle/waistband (i.e. 'belt'); بر is the preposition 'on/around'; میان is 'middle' — together: 'who, like him, does not (or did not) gird the belt around the middle.'
segments.s771-g123: پدر بر پدر is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'father upon father' (i.e. successive generations/ancestors); بر is the preposition 'upon/over' linking the repetition; داد is the past form of دادن 'gave' used here in a narrative sense to indicate succession or handing‑on; و is the conjunction 'and'; دانش‌پذیر functions as a predicate noun 'students/pupils' or 'those receptive to learning' describing the people in question.
segments.s771-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; نوشین‌روان is an epithet used as a proper name 'Nushin‑rōvān' ('sweet‑souled'); شاه is 'king' (the title attached to that epithet); تا here means 'as far as/until' and marks the range; اردشیر is the personal name 'Ardashir'. The whole phrase sets the span 'from Nushin‑rōvān the king to Ardashir.'
segments.s771-g125: بود is the past copula 'was'; اردشیرش is the name 'Ardashir' with the enclitic -ش ('his/its') attached — in epic Persian this often functions as a genitive/enclitic reference to the person already in view; به هشتم uses به plus an ordinal ('the eighth') to mark position in a descent; پدر here means 'ancestor/forefather' — so 'Ardashir was the eighth ancestor.'
segments.s771-g126: جهاندار is a compound title 'world‑holder/sovereign' (a royal epithet); ساسان is the proper name 'Sasan' (the ancestor/founder); با is the preposition 'with'; داد is the noun 'justice' (here an attribute); و is 'and'; فر is the key epic noun 'farr' meaning royal glory or divine splendour. Grammatically this string lists titles and virtues attached to the named ancestor.
segments.s771-g127: که here introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'; یزدانش is 'God' (with the enclitic -ش; 'his God' is a common poetic possessive but here functions as 'God' acting on behalf of the king); تاج is 'crown'; کیان is the Kayanid royal line ('of the Kayan kings'); برنهاد is the past verb 'placed upon/set on' (بر‑نهاد = put on). The clause says God placed the Kayan crown upon him.
segments.s771-g128: همه is the universal quantifier 'all'; شهریارانش is 'his rulers/kings' (شهریاران with the enclitic -ش); فرخ‌نژاد is an adjective 'well‑born, of noble birth' describing those rulers. Grammatically this is a short nominal clause 'all his kings (were) well‑born' with the copula implied.
segments.s771-g145: هر آنکس که is a free relative: هر (any/whichever) + آنکس (that person) + که (who/that) introduces the clause. با تو (with you) is a prepositional complement placed before the verb, and نِگوید is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + گوید) meaning 'does not say/speak'; درست functions adverbially after the verb meaning 'correctly/honestly'. Note the verb-final clause order typical of Persian.
segments.s771-g146: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative sequence: چنان (thus/so) + دان (an older imperative/optative 'consider/know'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. او دشمن جان تست is the predication: او (he) + دشمن جان (enemy of life/soul). تست is an archaic/copular form (old 2nd‑person copula/ending equivalent to 'is for you' / 'is yours' in classical usage) — grammatically a vestigial copula attached to the clause.
segments.s771-g147: تو بیماری اکنون is a nominal sentence with zero copula: تو (you) as subject and بیماری (sick/illness) as predicate, modified by اکنون (now). و connects the clauses. ما چون پزشک is a simile: ما (we) + چون (like/as) + پزشک (physician), preparing for the following descriptive phrase.
segments.s771-g148: پزشک خروشان is a noun + participial/attributive adjective: پزشک (physician) + خروشان (roaring/crying aloud) describing how they act; به marks accompaniment/instrument here (’with/through’), so به خونین سرشک means 'with bloody teardrops' — خونین (bloodstained/bloody) modifies سرشک (teardrop). Word order places modifiers after the head noun in the adjective slot.
segments.s771-g149: This is a comparative structure: تو (you) ... کمتری (are less) with از marking the standard of comparison. بنده بندگان is a genitive-like phrase 'a servant of servants' (emphatically low), and کمتری is the comparative predicate 'less/fewer' referring back to تو.
segments.s771-g150: به اندیشه دل is a locative/mental-phrase 'in the thought of the heart' (به + اندیشه + دل), i.e. 'in your heart's thinking'. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' and takes مهتری ('preeminence/being greater') as its semantic object: 'do not entertain greatness/superiority'. Note the imperative negative follows the locative phrase.
segments.s771-g151: همی (an adverb here meaning 'always' or emphatic 'never/at all') + کینه (hatred/grudge) is the object; با پاک یزدان marks the target 'toward/against the Pure God'. نهی here functions as an admonitory negative (classical negative imperative/prohibition), so the clause reads 'do not harbor hatred against the Pure God.'
segments.s771-g152: ز راه خرد is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/through the way of wisdom' (ز used poetically as 'through/by'). جوی is an imperative 'seek' (from جویدن/جویا شدن in classical usage) and takes تخت مهی ('the beautiful/majestic throne' — تخت modified by مهی) as its object. Word order is verb (جوی) + object (تخت مهی).
segments.s771-g105: به آتش (prepositional phrase) precedes the object and here functions adverbially ('into/with fire'). نهال دلت را is the definite object: نهال (sapling) + دل (heart) + enclitic -ت (your) + را (objective marker). مسوز is a negative imperative (m‑ prefix + سوز 'burn') meaning 'do not burn'. The phrase is a metaphor: 'do not let the tender shoot of your heart be burned.'
segments.s771-g106: مکن is an imperative negative ('do not'). Here it governs the following predicative adjective phrase: تیره این تاج گیتی‌فروز = 'make/leave dark this crown that lights the world.' In older/poetic Persian the verb 'کردن' ('to make') is omitted and the adjective (تیره) comes before the noun it would affect, so the sense is 'do not make this crown dark.' گیتی‌فروز is a compound epithet modifying تاج.
segments.s771-g107: سپاه پراگنده را is the definite object phrase 'the dispersed army' (سپاه + adjective پراگنده + را). گرد کن is an imperative construction: گرد (here verbal/adverbial root meaning 'together/around' used to indicate gathering) + کن (do) = 'gather (them).' The verb addresses the addressee to assemble the troops.
segments.s771-g108: وزین سان = 'in this dignified/grave manner' (وزین = grave/dignified; سان = manner/like). که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which/as' and گفتی here refers to 'what you said' (your speech). مگردان سخن is a negative imperative: مگردان ('do not turn back/withdraw') + سخن ('speech, words') = 'do not go back on your word.'
segments.s771-g109: ازیدر is an archaic/poetic adverbial (literally 'from here' / 'thereupon'). به پوزش is an adverbial use of به + پوزش meaning 'by/with apology' (the به is used adverbially). بر شاه رو = بر (toward) + شاه (king) + رو (imperative 'turn/face'), i.e. 'turn to the king.' The verb رو here is the command-form 'turn/face.'
segments.s771-g110: چو بینی is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' and بینی = 'you see' (2nd-person present). ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'to him' (referring to the king). بندگی ساز نو is an instruction: بندگی ('servitude, service') + ساز (imperative 'make' from ساختن, i.e. 'renew/make') + نو ('new') — literally 'make new servitude,' i.e. 'renew your service.'
segments.s771-g111: وزان جایگه = 'from/and at that place/position' (وزان = 'and/from that'; جایگه = 'place, station'). جنگ لشکر بسیچ = 'a host/army for battle, many in number' (جنگ qualifies the army as for battle; بسیچ intensifies as 'numerous'). This segment sets the scene/place and the sizable force.
segments.s771-g112: ز رای و ز پوزش uses ز (poetic 'from' / 'by') to list causes: 'from/by counsel and by apology.' میاسای is a jussive/imperative-form verb meaning 'be at rest/settle' (here intended negatively with the following هیچ): the command is essentially 'do not be at ease.' هیچ (here an adverbial intensifier) means 'at all/none,' so the whole means 'do not rest/settle at all because of counsel or apologies.'
segments.s771-g161: همه موبدان is a nominal phrase 'all the mobeds / all the priests' (subject or topical phrase). تا introduces a temporal clause 'until/when'; جهان شد سیاه is a small clause with شد (past of شدن) as the copular/auxiliary 'became' and سیاه as the predicative adjective 'dark/black'. Word order places the temporal clause right after the topical phrase.
segments.s771-g162: بر آیین خورشید is a locative/benefactive phrase 'upon/on the (ceremony/seat) of the sun' (بر + noun + genitive). بنشست is the past singular of نشستن 'sat' (poetic form with prefixed ب‑) and here precedes the subject — a common poetic inversion. ماه is the grammatical subject 'the moon' and follows the verb.
segments.s771-g163: بگفتند is a past plural reporting verb 'they said' (introducing reported speech). زین گونه is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrase 'in this way / thus' (زین here is an older demonstrative). با + کینه‌جوی addresses or marks relation 'to/with the vengeful one' — here 'to the revenge-seeker'.
segments.s771-g164: نبد is an archaic negative/existential form ('was not' / 'did not exist'); سوی یک موی is a locative/measure phrase 'toward a single hair' (an idiom meaning 'the slightest bit'); زان is a demonstrative 'from that' or 'of that', linking back to the preceding matter; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation/discussion' and is governed by زان.
segments.s771-g165: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; شب تیره شد is 'the night became dark' with شد as the past/copular 'became'. گفت با موبدان here is best read as '[he] spoke with the mobeds' — گفت functions as the verbal/noun center 'speech/conversation' followed by با + object.
segments.s771-g166: شما را is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun with the definite-object/topic marker را (here marking the person addressed). بباید شد is a periphrastic obligation: بباید 'must/ought' plus شد (become) gives the sense 'it must be for you' or 'you must (do this)'. ای بخردان is a vocative 'O wise ones' addressing the addressees.
segments.s771-g167: من امشب sets the agent and time 'I tonight'. بگردانم is a first‑person singular present/future form of گرداندن (poetic ب‑ prefix) meaning 'I will turn/send/dispatch/manage' (here 'I will send/dispatch'); این با پسر marks what and with whom: 'this with the son' (this thing will go with the son).
segments.s771-g168: ز هر گونه‌ای is a quantifying prepositional phrase 'from every kind/type'. دانش is the direct object 'knowledge'. آرم in the manuscript is used where we expect 'I will bring' (classical/poetic form equivalent to آورم); the provided sense list does not include 'bring', so the exact lexical sense in the provided list is not available. ببر is an imperative 'take!' (here telling the addressee to accept what is brought).
segments.s771-g49: تو‌گر is the conditional 'if you are' (an archaic/poetic fusion of تو اگر). بنده‌ای is a predicate noun 'a servant' with the -ای ending marking an indefinite/characterizing noun. خون شاهان is a genitive phrase 'the blood of kings' and مریز is a negative imperative from ریختن ('do not spill/let flow'), so the whole segment reads 'If you are a servant, do not spill the blood of kings.'
segments.s771-g50: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'. نفرین is the noun 'curse'. بود is the past/auxiliary form 'was/would be' here indicating the resulting state, and بر تو is the prepositional 'upon you'. تا is the temporal 'until' and رستخیز means 'the Resurrection' (the Last Judgment). Together: 'for a curse would be upon you until the Resurrection.'
segments.s771-g51: بگفت is a past tense verb 'he said'. این ('this') is the direct object referring to the preceding utterance. و simply links clauses 'and'. بنشست is 'he sat down' (past). گریان is a participial/adverbial 'weeping', and به درد here means 'in/with pain' (emotional sorrow). The clause therefore: 'He said this and sat down, weeping with pain.'
segments.s771-g52: پر از ... is the idiom 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). خون دل literally 'blood of the heart' (دل = heart) is a poetic image of a bleeding/tormented heart. و links the two images; مژه پر آب زرد literally 'eyelashes full of yellow water'—a poetic way to describe tear-filled eyes (آب = water/tears; زرد = yellow/pale).
segments.s771-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنشست 'sat down' (past). گریان is adverbial/participial 'weeping'. بشد is the past of شدن 'became/it happened' and introduces the result; مهرنوش is a proper name placed after the verb (inverted order) — 'Mehrnush became...'.
segments.s771-g54: پر از درد = 'full of pain' (پر = full, از links the measure). با ناله و با خروش uses با 'with' twice to list accompanying sounds: ناله 'moan/lament' and خروش 'cry/roar', describing the manner of her anguish.
segments.s771-g55: به marks the manner here ('in/by'), and ماهوی is used adjectivally/adverbially 'lofty' or 'spiritual' (a poetic register). گفت is the past 'he said'. ای is the vocative 'O', and بد بدنژاد is the vocative phrase 'ill/evil, of low birth' (بد = bad/ill; بدنژاد = low-born/ignoble). Altogether: 'He said in a lofty manner, "O ill-born one..."'.
segments.s771-g56: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. نه ... نه is correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. رای فرجام literally 'counsel/opinion about the outcome' (رای = counsel/plan, فرجام = end/outcome). دانی is the 2nd-person present/presumed 'you know' governing both items, and داد is the noun 'justice' (not a verb here). The clause thus: 'who knows neither counsel about the outcome nor justice.'
